│                                                                  │
│  NETWORKING                                                      │
│  ├─ Elastic IP (1, while instance running)      $0.00          │
│  ├─ Data Transfer Out (100 GB)                  $9.00          │
│  └─ Application Load Balancer                  $16.20          │
│                                            Subtotal: $25.20     │
│                                                                  │
│  DATABASE                                                        │
│  └─ RDS PostgreSQL (db.t3.medium, 1-yr Reserved) $34.00        │
│                                            Subtotal: $34.00     │
│                                                                  │
│  MONITORING & SECURITY                                           │
│  ├─ CloudWatch detailed monitoring               $2.10          │
│  ├─ CloudWatch Logs (10 GB)                      $5.00          │
│  ├─ CloudWatch Alarms (10 alarms)                $1.00          │
│  └─ AWS Backup                                   $5.00          │
│                                            Subtotal: $13.10     │
│                                                                  │
│  OTHER SERVICES                                                  │
│  ├─ Route 53 (1 hosted zone)                     $0.50          │
│  ├─ ACM SSL Certificate                          $0.00 (FREE)   │
│  ├─ SES (Email sending, 10K emails)              $1.00          │
│  └─ CloudFront CDN (50 GB)                       $4.25          │
│                                            Subtotal: $5.75      │
│                                                                  │
│  ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ │
│  TOTAL MONTHLY COST:                            $149.94/month   │
│  ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ │
│                                                                  │
│  💰 vs Non-Optimized (all On-Demand):          $287.40/month   │
│  💰 MONTHLY SAVINGS:                             $137.46        │
│  💰 ANNUAL SAVINGS:                            $1,649.52        │
│  💰 SAVINGS PERCENTAGE:                            48%          │
│                                                                  │
│  📈 Cost as your anihpj grows:                                   │
│  • 10,000 users/month:    ~$150-200/month                       │
│  • 50,000 users/month:    ~$300-400/month                       │
│  • 100,000 users/month:   ~$600-800/month                       │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💾 EBS Volume Type Comparison
Filter by Use Case:
Volume Type Storage Class Max IOPS Max Throughput Price per GB/month Best For Use Case
gp3 SSD 16,000 1,000 MB/s $0.08 ⭐ Most workloads Django apps, databases, boot volumes
gp2 SSD 16,000 250 MB/s $0.10 Legacy workloads Older applications (use gp3 instead)
io2 Provisioned IOPS SSD 64,000 1,000 MB/s $0.125 + IOPS cost Mission-critical Large databases, high-traffic apps
io2 Block Express Provisioned IOPS SSD 256,000 4,000 MB/s $0.125 + IOPS cost Extreme performance SAP HANA, large Oracle databases
st1 Throughput Optimized HDD 500 500 MB/s $0.045 Big data, logs Data warehouses, log processing
sc1 Cold HDD 250 250 MB/s $0.015 Infrequent access Backups, archives (lowest cost)

💡 Recommendation for Django Job Portal: Use gp3 for root volumes (20 GB) and application data. It's 20% cheaper than gp2 with better performance. For backups, consider sc1 for maximum cost savings.

📈 Scalability & High Availability

🔄 Auto Scaling for anihpj
🎯 Auto Scaling Architecture
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          AUTO SCALING GROUP ARCHITECTURE                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │           Application Load Balancer (ALB)                  │ │
│  │              anihpj-job-portal-alb                         │ │
│  │         Health checks: /health/ endpoint                   │ │
│  └──────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────── │ │
│                         │                                       │
│         ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐                      │
│         │               │               │                       │
│  ┌──────▼─────┐  ┌─────▼──────┐  ┌────▼───────┐              │
│  │ AZ-A       │  │ AZ-B       │  │ AZ-C       │              │
│  │            │  │            │  │            │              │
│  │ ┌────────┐ │  │ ┌────────┐ │  │ ┌────────┐ │              │
│  │ │EC2     │ │  │ │EC2     │ │  │ │EC2     │ │              │
│  │ │anihpj-1│ │  │ │anihpj-2│ │  │ │anihpj-3│ │              │
│  │ │m5.large│ │  │ │m5.large│ │  │ │m5.large│ │              │
│  │ └────────┘ │  │ └────────┘ │  │ └────────┘ │              │
│  └────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘              │
│                                                                  │
│  Auto Scaling Group: anihpj-asg                                 │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  Desired Capacity: 2 instances                             │ │
│  │  Minimum: 1 instance  (always at least 1 running)          │ │
│  │  Maximum: 6 instances (cost protection)                    │ │
│  │                                                             │ │
│  │  Health Check Type: ELB                                    │ │
│  │  Health Check Grace Period: 300 seconds                    │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│  Scaling Triggers:                                              │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  📈 SCALE OUT (Add instances)                               │ │
│  │  • CPU > 70% for 5 minutes  → Add 1 instance               │ │
│  │  • Memory > 80% for 5 min   → Add 1 instance               │ │
│  │  • Active connections > 1000 → Add 1 instance               │ │
│  │  • Scheduled: 8am weekdays  → Add 2 instances               │ │
│  │                                                             │ │
│  │  📉 SCALE IN (Remove instances)                             │ │
│  │  • CPU < 30% for 10 minutes → Remove 1 instance            │ │
│  │  • After business hours     → Remove 2 instances           │ │
│  │  • Weekends (low traffic)   → Scale to minimum             │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│  Benefits:                                                       │
│  ✅ Handle traffic spikes automatically                          │
│  ✅ Reduce costs during low-traffic periods                      │
│  ✅ Maintain performance and availability                        │
│  ✅ No manual intervention needed                                │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🚀 Create Launch Template
# Create launch template for anihpj
aws ec2 create-launch-template \
    --launch-template-name anihpj-launch-template \
    --launch-template-data '{
        "ImageId": "ami-0c7217cdde317cfec",
        "InstanceType": "m5.large",
        "KeyName": "anihpj-keypair",
        "IamInstanceProfile": {
            "Name": "anihpj-ec2-profile"
        },
        "SecurityGroupIds": ["sg-0abc123def456789"],
        "UserData": "'$(base64 -w 0 user-data.sh)'",
        "BlockDeviceMappings": [
            {
                "DeviceName": "/dev/sda1",
                "Ebs": {
                    "VolumeSize": 20,
                    "VolumeType": "gp3",
                    "DeleteOnTermination": true,
                    "Encrypted": true
                }
            }
        ],
        "TagSpecifications": [
            {
                "ResourceType": "instance",
                "Tags": [
                    {"Key": "Name", "Value": "anihpj-auto-scaled"},
                    {"Key": "Project", "Value": "anihpj"},
                    {"Key": "Environment", "Value": "production"},
                    {"Key": "ManagedBy", "Value": "AutoScaling"}
                ]
            }
        ],
        "Monitoring": {
            "Enabled": true
        }
    }'
⚙️ Create Auto Scaling Group
# Create Auto Scaling Group
aws autoscaling create-auto-scaling-group \
    --auto-scaling-group-name anihpj-asg \
    --launch-template LaunchTemplateName=anihpj-launch-template,Version='$Latest' \
    --min-size 1 \
    --max-size 6 \
    --desired-capacity 2 \
    --default-cooldown 300 \
    --health-check-type ELB \
    --health-check-grace-period 300 \
    --vpc-zone-identifier "subnet-0abc123,subnet-0def456,subnet-0ghi789" \
    --target-group-arns "arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:targetgroup/anihpj-tg/abc123" \
    --tags "Key=Name,Value=anihpj-asg-instance,PropagateAtLaunch=true" \
           "Key=Environment,Value=production,PropagateAtLaunch=true"

# Create scaling policies

# 1. Target Tracking Scaling (Recommended - Simple!)
aws autoscaling put-scaling-policy \
    --auto-scaling-group-name anihpj-asg \
    --policy-name anihpj-target-tracking-cpu \
    --policy-type TargetTrackingScaling \
    --target-tracking-configuration '{
        "PredefinedMetricSpecification": {
            "PredefinedMetricType": "ASGAverageCPUUtilization"
        },
        "TargetValue": 70.0
    }'

# 2. Step Scaling Policy (More control)
aws autoscaling put-scaling-policy \
    --auto-scaling-group-name anihpj-asg \
    --policy-name anihpj-scale-out-policy \
    --policy-type StepScaling \
    --adjustment-type ChangeInCapacity \
    --metric-aggregation-type Average \
    --step-adjustments \
        MetricIntervalLowerBound=0,MetricIntervalUpperBound=10,ScalingAdjustment=1 \
        MetricIntervalLowerBound=10,ScalingAdjustment=2

# 3. Scheduled Scaling (Predictable patterns)
# Scale up weekday mornings
aws autoscaling put-scheduled-update-group-action \
    --auto-scaling-group-name anihpj-asg \
    --scheduled-action-name anihpj-scale-up-morning \
    --recurrence "0 8 * * MON-FRI" \
    --desired-capacity 4

# Scale down weekday evenings
aws autoscaling put-scheduled-update-group-action \
    --auto-scaling-group-name anihpj-asg \
    --scheduled-action-name anihpj-scale-down-evening \
    --recurrence "0 18 * * MON-FRI" \
    --desired-capacity 2

# Scale to minimum on weekends
aws autoscaling put-scheduled-update-group-action \
    --auto-scaling-group-name anihpj-asg \
    --scheduled-action-name anihpj-weekend-minimum \
    --recurrence "0 0 * * SAT" \
    --desired-capacity 1
⚖️ Load Balancing
📊 Load Balancer Types Comparison
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│               ELASTIC LOAD BALANCER TYPES                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│  1. APPLICATION LOAD BALANCER (ALB) - Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS)      │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  ✅ Best for: Django web applications (anihpj)              │ │
│  │  ✅ Path-based routing: /api/* → API servers                │ │
│  │  ✅ Host-based routing: api.anihpj.com vs www.anihpj.com    │ │
│  │  ✅ SSL/TLS termination                                      │ │
│  │  ✅ WebSocket support                                        │ │
│  │  ✅ HTTP/2 support                                           │ │
│  │  ✅ Authentication (OIDC, Cognito)                           │ │
│  │  ✅ Request/response headers modification                    │ │
│  │  ✅ Lambda targets support                                   │ │
│  │                                                              │ │
│  │  Price: $0.0225/hour + $0.008/LCU-hour                     │ │
│  │        (~$16.20/month + data processed)                     │ │
│  │                                                              │ │
│  │  Use Case: anihpj Django application ← RECOMMENDED          │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│  2. NETWORK LOAD BALANCER (NLB) - Layer 4 (TCP/UDP)             │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  ✅ Ultra-low latency (microseconds)                         │ │
│  │  ✅ Millions of requests per second                          │ │
│  │  ✅ Static IP support                                        │ │
│  │  ✅ TCP, UDP, TLS protocols                                  │ │
│  │  ❌ No path-based routing                                    │ │
│  │  ❌ No HTTP header inspection                                │ │
│  │                                                              │ │
│  │  Use Case: Real-time gaming, IoT, high-performance apps    │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│  3. CLASSIC LOAD BALANCER (CLB) - Legacy                        │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  ⚠️ Legacy, use ALB or NLB instead                           │ │
│  │  Layer 4 & 7, but fewer features                           │ │
│  │  Being phased out                                           │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│  4. GATEWAY LOAD BALANCER (GWLB) - Layer 3                      │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  For virtual appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS)               │ │
│  │  Not typically used for web applications                   │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔧 Configure Application Load Balancer
# 1. Create Target Group
aws elbv2 create-target-group \
    --name anihpj-target-group \
    --protocol HTTP \
    --port 80 \
    --vpc-id vpc-0abc123 \
    --health-check-enabled \
    --health-check-path /health/ \
    --health-check-interval-seconds 30 \
    --health-check-timeout-seconds 5 \
    --healthy-threshold-count 2 \
    --unhealthy-threshold-count 3 \
    --matcher HttpCode=200

# 2. Create Application Load Balancer
aws elbv2 create-load-balancer \
    --name anihpj-alb \
    --subnets subnet-0abc123 subnet-0def456 subnet-0ghi789 \
    --security-groups sg-0alb123 \
    --scheme internet-facing \
    --type application \
    --ip-address-type ipv4 \
    --tags Key=Name,Value=anihpj-alb Key=Environment,Value=production

# Output will include: LoadBalancerArn and DNSName
# DNSName: anihpj-alb-1234567890.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com

# 3. Create HTTP Listener (redirect to HTTPS)
aws elbv2 create-listener \
    --load-balancer-arn arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:loadbalancer/app/anihpj-alb/abc123 \
    --protocol HTTP \
    --port 80 \
    --default-actions Type=redirect,RedirectConfig='{Protocol=HTTPS,Port=443,StatusCode=HTTP_301}'

# 4. Create HTTPS Listener
aws elbv2 create-listener \
    --load-balancer-arn arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:loadbalancer/app/anihpj-alb/abc123 \
    --protocol HTTPS \
    --port 443 \
    --certificates CertificateArn=arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:123456789012:certificate/abc123 \
    --ssl-policy ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS-1-2-2017-01 \
    --default-actions Type=forward,TargetGroupArn=arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:targetgroup/anihpj-tg/abc123

# 5. Create path-based routing rules (advanced)
# Route /api/* to API servers
aws elbv2 create-rule \
    --listener-arn arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:listener/app/anihpj-alb/abc123/def456 \
    --priority 10 \
    --conditions Field=path-pattern,Values='/api/*' \
    --actions Type=forward,TargetGroupArn=arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:targetgroup/anihpj-api-tg/xyz789

# Route /static/* to static file servers or S3
aws elbv2 create-rule \
    --listener-arn arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:listener/app/anihpj-alb/abc123/def456 \
    --priority 20 \
    --conditions Field=path-pattern,Values='/static/*' \
    --actions Type=redirect,RedirectConfig='{Protocol=HTTPS,Host=static.anihpj.com,StatusCode=HTTP_301}'
🏥 Django Health Check Endpoint
# anihpj/jobpost/views.py
from django.http import JsonResponse
from django.db import connection
from django.core.cache import cache
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def health_check(request):
    """
    Health check endpoint for ALB
    Returns 200 if healthy, 503 if unhealthy
    """
    health_status = {
        'status': 'healthy',
        'checks': {}
    }
    is_healthy = True
    
    # Check database connectivity
    try:
        with connection.cursor() as cursor:
            cursor.execute("SELECT 1")
            health_status['checks']['database'] = 'ok'
    except Exception as e:
        health_status['checks']['database'] = f'error: {str(e)}'
        is_healthy = False
        logger.error(f"Database health check failed: {e}")
    
    # Check cache connectivity
    try:
        cache.set('health_check', 'ok', 10)
        if cache.get('health_check') == 'ok':
            health_status['checks']['cache'] = 'ok'
        else:
            health_status['checks']['cache'] = 'error'
            is_healthy = False
    except Exception as e:
        health_status['checks']['cache'] = f'error: {str(e)}'
        is_healthy = False
        logger.error(f"Cache health check failed: {e}")
    
    # Check disk space
    import shutil
    try:
        stat = shutil.disk_usage('/')
        free_percent = (stat.free / stat.total) * 100
        if free_percent < 10:
            health_status['checks']['disk'] = f'warning: {free_percent:.1f}% free'
            is_healthy = False
        else:
            health_status['checks']['disk'] = f'ok: {free_percent:.1f}% free'
    except Exception as e:
        health_status['checks']['disk'] = f'error: {str(e)}'
        is_healthy = False
    
    if is_healthy:
        return JsonResponse(health_status, status=200)
    else:
        health_status['status'] = 'unhealthy'
        return JsonResponse(health_status, status=503)

# anihpj/urls.py
from django.urls import path
from jobpost.views import health_check

urlpatterns = [
    # ... other patterns ...
    path('health/', health_check, name='health_check'),
]
🏗️ High Availability Architecture
🌐 Multi-AZ Deployment Pattern
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           HIGH AVAILABILITY ARCHITECTURE FOR ANIHPJ              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│                        ┌─────────────────┐                      │
│                        │   Route 53 DNS  │                      │
│                        │  anihpj.com     │                      │
│                        └────────┬────────┘                      │
│                                 │                                │
│                 ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐               │
│                 │                               │               │
│         ┌───────▼──────┐                ┌──────▼────────┐      │
│         │  CloudFront  │                │   WAF (Web    │      │
│         │     CDN      │                │   Firewall)   │      │
│         └───────┬──────┘                └──────┬────────┘      │
│                 │                               │               │
│                 └───────────────┬───────────────┘               │
│                                 │                                │
│                    ┌────────────▼──────────────┐                │
│                    │   Application Load        │                │
│                    │   Balancer (ALB)          │                │
│                    │   Multi-AZ                │                │
│                    └────────────┬──────────────┘                │
│                                 │                                │
│          ┌──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┐        │
│          │                      │                      │        │
│  ┌───────▼────────┐    ┌───────▼────────┐   ┌────────▼──────┐ │
│  │ Availability   │    │ Availability   │   │ Availability  │ │
│  │ Zone A         │    │ Zone B         │   │ Zone C        │ │
│  │                │    │                │   │               │ │
│  │ ┌────────────┐ │    │ ┌────────────┐ │   │ ┌───────────┐│ │
│  │ │ EC2 Web    │ │    │ │ EC2 Web    │ │   │ │ EC2 Web   ││ │
│  │ │ anihpj-1   │ │    │ │ anihpj-2   │ │   │ │ anihpj-3  ││ │
│  │ │ (Active)   │ │    │ │ (Active)   │ │   │ │ (Standby) ││ │
│  │ └────────────┘ │    │ └────────────┘ │   │ └───────────┘│ │
│  │                │    │                │   │               │ │
│  │ ┌────────────┐ │    │ ┌────────────┐ │   │               │ │
│  │ │ ElastiCache│ │    │ │ ElastiCache│ │   │               │ │
│  │ │ Redis      │ │    │ │ Redis      │ │   │               │ │
│  │ │ (Primary)  │ │    │ │ (Replica)  │ │   │               │ │
│  │ └────────────┘ │    │ └────────────┘ │   │               │ │
│  └────────────────┘    └────────────────┘   └───────────────┘ │
│          │                      │                      │        │
│          └──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┘        │
│                                 │                                │
│                    ┌────────────▼──────────────┐                │
│                    │   RDS PostgreSQL          │                │
│                    │   Multi-AZ                │                │
│                    │   Primary: AZ-A           │                │
│                    │   Standby: AZ-B           │                │
│                    │   Auto Failover           │                │
│                    └────────────┬──────────────┘                │
│                                 │                                │
│                    ┌────────────▼──────────────┐                │
│                    │   S3 Buckets              │                │
│                    │   - Static files          │                │
│                    │   - User uploads          │                │
│                    │   Versioning enabled      │                │
│                    └───────────────────────────┘                │
│                                                                  │
│  Benefits:                                                       │
│  ✅ 99.99% uptime SLA                                            │
│  ✅ Automatic failover                                           │
│  ✅ No single point of failure                                   │
│  ✅ Disaster recovery built-in                                   │
│  ✅ Geographic redundancy                                        │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 HA Best Practices for anihpj:
  • Deploy across at least 2 Availability Zones
  • Use Multi-AZ RDS for database
  • Store session data in ElastiCache, not local disk
  • Use S3 for static files and user uploads
  • Implement health checks on all services
  • Set up automated backups and disaster recovery

🎓 Advanced Topics

🚀 EC2 Fleet

Launch and manage thousands of EC2 instances with a single request. Mix On-Demand, Reserved, and Spot instances.

# Create EC2 Fleet for anihpj batch processing
aws ec2 create-fleet \
    --launch-template-configs '[
        {
            "LaunchTemplateSpecification": {
                "LaunchTemplateId": "lt-0abc123",
                "Version": "$Latest"
            },
            "Overrides": [
                {"InstanceType": "m5.large", "SubnetId": "subnet-0abc123"},
                {"InstanceType": "m5.xlarge", "SubnetId": "subnet-0def456"}
            ]
        }
    ]' \
    --target-capacity-specification '{
        "TotalTargetCapacity": 10,
        "OnDemandTargetCapacity": 2,
        "SpotTargetCapacity": 8,
        "DefaultTargetCapacityType": "spot"
    }' \
    --spot-options '{
        "AllocationStrategy": "diversified",
        "InstanceInterruptionBehavior": "terminate"
    }'
⚡ Enhanced Networking & Placement
🔌 Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA) for HPC
EFA provides lower latency and higher throughput than TCP transport
for inter-instance communication.

Use cases:
• Machine learning training (distributed)
• Computational fluid dynamics
• Weather modeling
• Financial simulations

Not needed for typical Django web applications like anihpj.
🎮 GPU Instances for ML Workloads
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              GPU INSTANCE TYPES FOR ML/AI                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│  P4 Instances (Latest generation - A100 GPUs)                   │
│  • p4d.24xlarge: 8× A100 GPUs, $32.77/hour                      │
│  • Use for: Large language models, deep learning training       │
│                                                                  │
│  P3 Instances (V100 GPUs)                                       │
│  • p3.2xlarge: 1× V100, $3.06/hour                              │
│  • Use for: ML training, inference                              │
│                                                                  │
│  G4 Instances (T4 GPUs - Cost-effective)                        │
│  • g4dn.xlarge: 1× T4, $0.526/hour                              │
│  • Use for: ML inference, video transcoding                     │
│                                                                  │
│  For anihpj Job Portal:                                         │
│  If implementing resume parsing with ML or job matching:        │
│  • Use g4dn.xlarge for inference                                │
│  • Use Spot instances for training (save 70%)                   │
│  • Store models in S3                                           │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🐳 Container Services Integration
📦 Running Django Containers on EC2
# Option 1: Docker on EC2 (Simple)
# Install Docker on EC2 instance
sudo yum update -y
sudo yum install -y docker
sudo service docker start
sudo usermod -a -G docker ec2-user

# Build and run anihpj container
docker build -t anihpj:latest .
docker run -d -p 80:8000 \
    -e DATABASE_URL=postgresql://... \
    -e SECRET_KEY=... \
    --name anihpj-container \
    anihpj:latest

# Option 2: ECS (Elastic Container Service) on EC2
# More managed, integrates with ALB, Auto Scaling
aws ecs create-cluster --cluster-name anihpj-cluster

# Option 3: EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service)
# For complex microservices architectures
# Overkill for single Django application
💤 EC2 Hibernation
Hibernation saves RAM to EBS, faster boot than cold start.

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Standard Stop/Start:                              │
│  Stop → 30-60 seconds → Start                      │
│  • OS boots from scratch                           │
│  • Applications restart                            │
│  • Cache rebuilds                                  │
│                                                     │
│  With Hibernation:                                 │
│  Hibernate → 5-10 seconds → Resume                 │
│  • RAM restored from EBS                           │
│  • Applications resume instantly                   │
│  • Cache preserved                                 │
│                                                     │
│  Use for anihpj:                                   │
│  • Dev/staging environments (save costs)           │
│  • Preserve application state                      │
│  • Faster startup for scheduled workloads          │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# Enable hibernation
aws ec2 run-instances \
    --image-id ami-0c7217cdde317cfec \
    --instance-type m5.large \
    --hibernation-options Configured=true \
    --block-device-mappings '[
        {
            "DeviceName": "/dev/xvda",
            "Ebs": {
                "VolumeSize": 30,
                "Encrypted": true
            }
        }
    ]'
⚙️ Nitro System
AWS Nitro System is the underlying platform for modern EC2 instances.

Benefits:
✅ Better performance (dedicated hardware for networking, storage)
✅ Enhanced security (hypervisor offloaded to dedicated hardware)
✅ More instance sizes and types
✅ Bare metal instances available
✅ Better price/performance

All modern instance types (M5, C5, R5, T3, etc.) use Nitro System.

For anihpj: Always choose Nitro-based instances (t3, m5, c5, etc.)
over previous generation (t2, m4, c4).

✨ Best Practices

🔒 Security Checklist
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           SECURITY BEST PRACTICES CHECKLIST                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│  IDENTITY & ACCESS                                               │
│  ☐ Use IAM roles, never store credentials on EC2                │
│  ☐ Enable MFA for all AWS accounts                              │
│  ☐ Apply principle of least privilege                           │
│  ☐ Rotate IAM credentials regularly                             │
│  ☐ Use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager instead of SSH       │
│                                                                  │
│  NETWORK SECURITY                                                │
│  ☐ Use Security Groups (stateful firewall)                      │
│  ☐ Restrict inbound traffic to necessary ports only             │
│  ☐ Use VPC with private subnets for databases                   │
│  ☐ Enable VPC Flow Logs for traffic analysis                    │
│  ☐ Use AWS WAF for web application firewall                     │
│  ☐ Enable DDoS protection with Shield Standard/Advanced         │
│                                                                  │
│  DATA PROTECTION                                                 │
│  ☐ Enable EBS encryption for all volumes                        │
│  ☐ Enable encryption at rest for RDS databases                  │
│  ☐ Use SSL/TLS for data in transit                              │
│  ☐ Enable S3 bucket encryption                                  │
│  ☐ Use AWS KMS for key management                               │
│  ☐ Enable CloudTrail for audit logging                          │
│                                                                  │
│  PATCHING & UPDATES                                              │
│  ☐ Enable automatic security updates                            │
│  ☐ Use AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager                        │
│  ☐ Keep Django and dependencies updated                         │
│  ☐ Regularly scan for vulnerabilities                           │
│  ☐ Use immutable infrastructure (replace, not patch)            │
│                                                                  │
│  MONITORING & INCIDENT RESPONSE                                  │
│  ☐ Enable CloudWatch alarms for suspicious activity             │
│  ☐ Enable GuardDuty for threat detection                        │
│  ☐ Set up SNS notifications for security events                 │
│  ☐ Implement automated incident response                        │
│  ☐ Regular security audits and penetration testing              │
│                                                                  │
│  BACKUP & DISASTER RECOVERY                                      │
│  ☐ Enable automated EBS snapshots                               │
│  ☐ Enable RDS automated backups                                 │
│  ☐ Test recovery procedures regularly                           │
│  ☐ Use cross-region replication for critical data               │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚡ Performance Optimization
🎯 Django Performance on EC2
# 1. Use connection pooling for database
# settings.py
DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.postgresql',
        'NAME': 'anihpj',
        'USER': 'anihpj_user',
        'PASSWORD': os.environ['DB_PASSWORD'],
        'HOST': 'anihpj-db.abc123.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com',
        'PORT': '5432',
        'CONN_MAX_AGE': 600,  # Connection pooling
        'OPTIONS': {
            'connect_timeout': 10,
            'keepalives': 1,
            'keepalives_idle': 30,
        }
    }
}

# 2. Implement caching with ElastiCache
CACHES = {
    'default': {
        'BACKEND': 'django_redis.cache.RedisCache',
        'LOCATION': 'redis://anihpj-redis.abc123.cache.amazonaws.com:6379/1',
        'OPTIONS': {
            'CLIENT_CLASS': 'django_redis.client.DefaultClient',
            'CONNECTION_POOL_KWARGS': {
                'max_connections': 50,
                'retry_on_timeout': True
            },
            'SOCKET_CONNECT_TIMEOUT': 5,
            'SOCKET_TIMEOUT': 5,
        }
    }
}

# Cache expensive queries
from django.core.cache import cache
from jobpost.models import JobPost

def get_featured_jobs():
    cache_key = 'featured_jobs'
    jobs = cache.get(cache_key)
    if not jobs:
        jobs = JobPost.objects.filter(
            is_featured=True
        ).select_related('author', 'location').prefetch_related('skills')[:10]
        cache.set(cache_key, jobs, 3600)  # Cache for 1 hour
    return jobs

# 3. Use database query optimization
# Bad - N+1 queries
jobs = JobPost.objects.all()
for job in jobs:
    print(job.author.name)  # Additional query per job!

# Good - Single query with JOIN
jobs = JobPost.objects.select_related('author', 'location').all()
for job in jobs:
    print(job.author.name)  # No additional query

# 4. Implement CDN for static files (CloudFront + S3)
# settings.py
AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME = 'anihpj-static'
AWS_S3_CUSTOM_DOMAIN = f'{AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME}.s3.amazonaws.com'
AWS_CLOUDFRONT_DOMAIN = 'd123456789abcd.cloudfront.net'

STATIC_URL = f'https://{AWS_CLOUDFRONT_DOMAIN}/static/'
MEDIA_URL = f'https://{AWS_CLOUDFRONT_DOMAIN}/media/'

# 5. Use Gunicorn with optimal workers
# gunicorn_config.py
import multiprocessing

bind = "0.0.0.0:8000"
workers = multiprocessing.cpu_count() * 2 + 1  # 2n + 1
worker_class = "gevent"  # Async workers
worker_connections = 1000
keepalive = 5
max_requests = 1000  # Restart workers after 1000 requests
max_requests_jitter = 50
timeout = 30
graceful_timeout = 30
📊 EC2 Instance Right-Sizing
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          EC2 RIGHT-SIZING WORKFLOW                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│  Step 1: Collect Metrics (2-4 weeks)                            │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  Monitor with CloudWatch:                                   │ │
│  │  • CPU utilization (average, peak)                          │ │
│  │  • Memory utilization                                       │ │
│  │  • Network throughput                                       │ │
│  │  • Disk I/O                                                 │ │
│  │  • Application response times                               │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                         │                                        │
│  Step 2: Analyze Usage Patterns                                 │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  If average CPU < 30% → Downsize instance                  │ │
│  │  If average CPU > 80% → Upsize instance                    │ │
│  │  If memory constantly high → Choose memory-optimized       │ │
│  │  If disk I/O bottleneck → Use provisioned IOPS EBS         │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                         │                                        │
│  Step 3: Test New Size (Staging)                                │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  • Launch new instance with target size                    │ │
│  │  • Run load tests                                           │ │
│  │  • Compare performance and cost                            │ │
│  │  • Verify acceptable performance                           │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                         │                                        │
│  Step 4: Implement Change (Production)                          │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  • Schedule maintenance window                              │ │
│  │  • Update Launch Template with new instance type           │ │
│  │  • Trigger Auto Scaling replacement                         │ │
│  │  • Monitor closely for 24-48 hours                          │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                         │                                        │
│  Step 5: Continuous Optimization                                │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  • Review monthly with AWS Cost Explorer                    │ │
│  │  • Use AWS Compute Optimizer recommendations               │ │
│  │  • Adjust based on growth                                   │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│  Example for anihpj:                                            │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  Current: m5.large (2 vCPU, 8 GB RAM) - $70/month          │ │
│  │  Metrics: Average CPU 25%, Average Memory 45%              │ │
│  │  Recommendation: t3.medium (2 vCPU, 4 GB RAM) - $30/month │ │
│  │  Savings: $40/month × 3 instances = $120/month saved      │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💾 Backup & Disaster Recovery
🔄 Automated Backup Strategy
# 1. Automated EBS Snapshots with DLM (Data Lifecycle Manager)
aws dlm create-lifecycle-policy \
    --execution-role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AWSDataLifecycleManagerDefaultRole \
    --description "Daily snapshots for anihpj EC2 volumes" \
    --state ENABLED \
    --policy-details '{
        "ResourceTypes": ["VOLUME"],
        "TargetTags": [
            {"Key": "Project", "Value": "anihpj"}
        ],
        "Schedules": [
            {
                "Name": "Daily snapshots",
                "CreateRule": {
                    "Interval": 24,
                    "IntervalUnit": "HOURS",
                    "Times": ["03:00"]
                },
                "RetainRule": {
                    "Count": 7
                },
                "TagsToAdd": [
                    {"Key": "SnapshotType", "Value": "DailyBackup"}
                ],
                "CopyTags": true
            }
        ]
    }'

# 2. Create AMI for disaster recovery
aws ec2 create-image \
    --instance-id i-0abc123def456789 \
    --name "anihpj-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)" \
    --description "Backup AMI for disaster recovery" \
    --no-reboot  # Don't reboot instance

# 3. Copy snapshots to different region (DR)
aws ec2 copy-snapshot \
    --source-region us-east-1 \
    --source-snapshot-id snap-0abc123def456789 \
    --destination-region us-west-2 \
    --description "DR snapshot for anihpj"
🚨 Disaster Recovery Plan
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         DISASTER RECOVERY TIERS FOR ANIHPJ                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│  Tier 1: Backup & Restore (Lowest cost, slowest recovery)       │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  RTO: 24 hours  |  RPO: 24 hours                           │ │
│  │  • Daily EBS snapshots                                      │ │
│  │  • Daily database backups to S3                            │ │
│  │  • Restore from snapshots when disaster occurs             │ │
│  │  Cost: ~$50/month (storage only)                           │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│  Tier 2: Pilot Light (Medium cost, medium recovery)             │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  RTO: 1-2 hours  |  RPO: 1 hour                            │ │
│  │  • Minimal resources running in DR region                  │ │
│  │  • Database standby replica (read-only)                    │ │
│  │  • AMIs and snapshots ready                                │ │
│  │  • Scale up EC2 instances when disaster occurs             │ │
│  │  Cost: ~$200/month (small DB + snapshots)                  │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│  Tier 3: Warm Standby (Higher cost, faster recovery)            │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  RTO: 15-30 minutes  |  RPO: 5-15 minutes                  │ │
│  │  • Scaled-down version running in DR region                │ │
│  │  • 1 EC2 instance running (instead of 3)                   │ │
│  │  • Database with continuous replication                    │ │
│  │  • Route 53 health checks for automatic failover           │ │
│  │  Cost: ~$500/month                                          │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│  Tier 4: Hot Standby (Highest cost, fastest recovery)           │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  RTO: < 5 minutes  |  RPO: Real-time                       │ │
│  │  • Full production environment in DR region                │ │
│  │  • Active-active configuration                              │ │
│  │  • Route 53 distributes traffic to both regions            │ │
│  │  • Database with synchronous replication                   │ │
│  │  Cost: ~$1000/month (2× production costs)                  │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│  Recommendation for anihpj:                                     │
│  Start with Tier 2 (Pilot Light) - Best balance of cost/RTO    │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🏷️ Tagging Strategy
Consistent tagging is crucial for cost allocation, automation, and management.

Required Tags for anihpj:
┌─────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Tag Key         │ Example Value                            │
├─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Name            │ anihpj-web-server-01                     │
│ Project         │ anihpj                                   │
│ Environment     │ production / staging / development       │
│ Owner           │ devops-team@company.com                  │
│ CostCenter      │ engineering                              │
│ Application     │ job-portal                               │
│ ManagedBy       │ terraform / cloudformation / manual      │
│ BackupSchedule  │ daily / weekly / none                    │
│ DataClass       │ public / internal / confidential         │
│ ComplianceReq   │ none / pci-dss / hipaa / gdpr           │
└─────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────┘

# Apply tags using AWS CLI
aws ec2 create-tags \
    --resources i-0abc123def456789 \
    --tags \
        Key=Name,Value=anihpj-web-server-01 \
        Key=Project,Value=anihpj \
        Key=Environment,Value=production \
        Key=Owner,Value=devops@company.com \
        Key=CostCenter,Value=engineering

💼 Common Use Cases

🌐 3-Tier Web Application (anihpj)
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         3-TIER ARCHITECTURE FOR ANIHPJ JOB PORTAL                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│  ┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ │
│  ┃ TIER 1: PRESENTATION LAYER (Public Subnets)                ┃ │
│  ┗━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┛ │
│                                                                  │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  Internet Gateway                                         │  │
│  └────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                           │                                     │
│  ┌────────────────────────▼─────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  CloudFront CDN (Global Edge Locations)                  │  │
│  │  • Cache static content (CSS, JS, images)                │  │
│  │  • SSL/TLS termination                                   │  │
│  │  • DDoS protection                                       │  │
│  └────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                           │                                     │
│  ┌────────────────────────▼─────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  Application Load Balancer (ALB)                         │  │
│  │  anihpj-alb.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com                  │  │
│  │  • HTTPS listener (port 443)                             │  │
│  │  • Path-based routing                                    │  │
│  │  • Health checks                                         │  │
│  └──────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                     │                                           │
│  ┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━▼━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ │
│  ┃ TIER 2: APPLICATION LAYER (Private Subnets)              ┃ │
│  ┗━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┛ │
│                                                                  │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐     │
│  │ EC2 Instance │    │ EC2 Instance │    │ EC2 Instance │     │
│  │   (AZ-A)     │    │   (AZ-B)     │    │   (AZ-C)     │     │
│  ├──────────────┤    ├──────────────┤    ├──────────────┤     │
│  │ Django       │    │ Django       │    │ Django       │     │
│  │ Gunicorn     │    │ Gunicorn     │    │ Gunicorn     │     │
│  │ anihpj app   │    │ anihpj app   │    │ anihpj app   │     │
│  │              │    │              │    │              │     │
│  │ m5.large     │    │ m5.large     │    │ m5.large     │     │
│  └──────┬───────┘    └──────┬───────┘    └──────┬───────┘     │
│         │                    │                    │             │
│         └────────────────────┼────────────────────┘             │
│                              │                                  │
│  ┌──────────────────────────▼──────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │  ElastiCache Redis Cluster (Private Subnets)           │   │
│  │  • Session storage                                      │   │
│  │  • Cache layer                                          │   │
│  │  • Primary + 2 replicas (Multi-AZ)                      │   │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                              │                                  │
│  ┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━▼━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓ │
│  ┃ TIER 3: DATA LAYER (Private Subnets)                    ┃ │
│  ┗━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┛ │
│                                                                  │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  RDS PostgreSQL (Multi-AZ)                             │    │
│  │  • Primary instance (AZ-A): anihpj-db-master           │    │
│  │  • Standby replica (AZ-B): automatic failover          │    │
│  │  • Automated backups (daily snapshots)                 │    │
│  │  • db.m5.large (2 vCPU, 8 GB RAM)                      │    │
│  │  • 100 GB SSD storage (gp3)                            │    │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                  │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  S3 Buckets                                             │    │
│  │  • anihpj-static: CSS, JS, images (CloudFront origin)  │    │
│  │  • anihpj-media: User uploads (resumes, logos)         │    │
│  │  • anihpj-backups: Database exports, logs              │    │
│  │  Versioning: Enabled | Encryption: Enabled             │    │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                  │
│  Benefits of 3-Tier Architecture:                               │
│  ✅ High availability (Multi-AZ deployment)                      │
│  ✅ Scalability (independent scaling of each tier)               │
│  ✅ Security (layers of isolation)                               │
│  ✅ Performance (caching, load balancing)                        │
│  ✅ Cost-effective (right-sized instances per tier)              │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚙️ Batch Processing
📊 Resume Parsing with AWS Batch
# Use case: Parse uploaded resumes in batch (PDF → structured data)
# Architecture: S3 upload → Lambda trigger → AWS Batch job

# jobpost/batch_processing.py
import boto3
import PyPDF2
import json
from datetime import datetime

def process_resume_batch(s3_bucket, s3_keys):
    """
    Process multiple resumes in batch
    Runs on EC2 Spot instances via AWS Batch
    """
    s3 = boto3.client('s3')
    results = []
    
    for key in s3_keys:
        try:
            # Download resume from S3
            response = s3.get_object(Bucket=s3_bucket, Key=key)
            pdf_content = response['Body'].read()
            
            # Parse PDF
            pdf_reader = PyPDF2.PdfReader(io.BytesIO(pdf_content))
            text = ''
            for page in pdf_reader.pages:
                text += page.extract_text()
            
            # Extract structured data (simplified)
            parsed_data = {
                'filename': key,
                'text': text,
                'email': extract_email(text),
                'phone': extract_phone(text),
                'skills': extract_skills(text),
                'experience_years': extract_experience(text),
                'processed_at': datetime.utcnow().isoformat()
            }
            
            # Store results in S3 or database
            result_key = key.replace('uploads/', 'parsed/').replace('.pdf', '.json')
            s3.put_object(
                Bucket=s3_bucket,
                Key=result_key,
                Body=json.dumps(parsed_data),
                ContentType='application/json'
            )
            
            results.append({'key': key, 'status': 'success'})
            
        except Exception as e:
            results.append({'key': key, 'status': 'error', 'error': str(e)})
    
    return results

# AWS Batch configuration
# Runs on Spot instances (70% cost savings)
# Scales from 0 to 100 instances based on queue depth
💻 Dev/Test Environments
# Cost-effective dev/test strategy for anihpj

# 1. Use smaller instance types
# Production: m5.large ($70/month)
# Dev/Test: t3.medium ($30/month) - Save $40/month

# 2. Use Spot Instances (save 70%)
# Dev/Test: t3.medium Spot ($9/month) - Save $61/month

# 3. Automate start/stop schedules
# Only run dev instances during business hours (8am-6pm, Mon-Fri)
# Cost: $9/month × 50 hours/week ÷ 168 hours/week = $2.68/month

# Lambda function to stop dev instances at 6pm
import boto3
from datetime import datetime

def lambda_handler(event, context):
    ec2 = boto3.client('ec2')
    
    # Stop all instances tagged Environment=development
    response = ec2.describe_instances(
        Filters=[
            {'Name': 'tag:Environment', 'Values': ['development']},
            {'Name': 'instance-state-name', 'Values': ['running']}
        ]
    )
    
    instance_ids = []
    for reservation in response['Reservations']:
        for instance in reservation['Instances']:
            instance_ids.append(instance['InstanceId'])
    
    if instance_ids:
        ec2.stop_instances(InstanceIds=instance_ids)
        print(f"Stopped instances: {instance_ids}")
    
    return {'statusCode': 200, 'body': 'Development instances stopped'}

# Schedule with EventBridge: 0 18 * * MON-FRI (6pm weekdays)

🔧 Troubleshooting & FAQs

❌ Instance Won't Start
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         TROUBLESHOOTING: INSTANCE WON'T START                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│  Problem: Instance stuck in "pending" state                      │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  Possible causes:                                           │ │
│  │  1. Insufficient capacity in Availability Zone              │ │
│  │  2. Instance limit reached                                  │ │
│  │  3. Invalid AMI                                             │ │
│  │                                                              │ │
│  │  Solutions:                                                  │ │
│  │  • Try different Availability Zone                          │ │
│  │  • Request service limit increase                           │ │
│  │  • Verify AMI is available in your region                   │ │
│  │  • Check System Status and Instance Status checks           │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│  Problem: Instance failing status checks                         │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  System Status Check Failed (AWS infrastructure problem)   │ │
│  │  ✅ Solution: Stop and start instance (moves to new host)   │ │
│  │                                                              │ │
│  │  Instance Status Check Failed (OS/application problem)      │ │
│  │  ✅ Solution: Check system logs, fix OS issues              │ │
│  │  • Incorrect network configuration                          │ │
│  │  • Kernel panic                                             │ │
│  │  • Out of memory                                            │ │
│  │  • Corrupt filesystem                                       │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│  Problem: Instance terminated unexpectedly                       │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  Check termination reason:                                  │ │
│  │  • Spot Instance interrupted (check Spot request status)    │ │
│  │  • Auto Scaling scaled in (check ASG activity history)      │ │
│  │  • User terminated (check CloudTrail logs)                  │ │
│  │  • Health check failed (check ELB target health)            │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# Check instance status
aws ec2 describe-instance-status --instance-ids i-0abc123def456789

# View system logs
aws ec2 get-console-output --instance-id i-0abc123def456789

# Reboot instance
aws ec2 reboot-instances --instance-ids i-0abc123def456789

# Stop and start (moves to new physical host)
aws ec2 stop-instances --instance-ids i-0abc123def456789
aws ec2 start-instances --instance-ids i-0abc123def456789
🔌 Can't Connect to Instance
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         TROUBLESHOOTING: CONNECTION ISSUES                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│  SSH Connection Timeout                                          │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  Checklist:                                                  │ │
│  │  ☐ Instance is running (not stopped/terminated)             │ │
│  │  ☐ Instance has public IP address                           │ │
│  │  ☐ Security Group allows inbound SSH (port 22)              │ │
│  │  ☐ Network ACL allows traffic                               │ │
│  │  ☐ Route table has route to Internet Gateway                │ │
│  │  ☐ Correct key pair is being used                           │ │
│  │  ☐ Key file permissions are correct (chmod 400)             │ │
│  │                                                              │ │
│  │  Debug command:                                              │ │
│  │  ssh -vvv -i anihpj-keypair.pem ec2-user@1.2.3.4           │ │
│  │  (Verbose output shows where connection fails)              │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│  Permission Denied (publickey)                                   │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  • Wrong username (ec2-user for Amazon Linux, ubuntu for   │ │
│  │    Ubuntu, admin for Debian)                                │ │
│  │  • Wrong key pair                                           │ │
│  │  • Key file permissions too open (must be 400)              │ │
│  │                                                              │ │
│  │  Fix key permissions:                                        │ │
│  │  chmod 400 anihpj-keypair.pem                               │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
│  Application Not Accessible (HTTP/HTTPS)                         │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  ☐ Security Group allows inbound port 80/443                │ │
│  │  ☐ Django is running (check with: ps aux | grep gunicorn)  │ │
│  │  ☐ Django is listening on 0.0.0.0 (not 127.0.0.1)          │ │
│  │  ☐ Firewall allows traffic (sudo iptables -L)               │ │
│  │  ☐ ALLOWED_HOSTS in Django settings includes EC2 IP         │ │
│  │                                                              │ │
│  │  Test locally on EC2:                                        │ │
│  │  curl http://localhost:8000                                  │ │
│  │  If works → Security Group issue                            │ │
│  │  If fails → Django configuration issue                      │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🐌 Performance Issues
📉 Diagnose Slow Performance
# 1. Check CPU utilization
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
    --namespace AWS/EC2 \
    --metric-name CPUUtilization \
    --dimensions Name=InstanceId,Value=i-0abc123def456789 \
    --start-time 2024-01-01T00:00:00Z \
    --end-time 2024-01-01T23:59:59Z \
    --period 3600 \
    --statistics Average,Maximum

# 2. Check memory usage (requires CloudWatch agent)
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
    --namespace CWAgent \
    --metric-name mem_used_percent \
    --dimensions Name=InstanceId,Value=i-0abc123def456789 \
    --start-time 2024-01-01T00:00:00Z \
    --end-time 2024-01-01T23:59:59Z \
    --period 3600 \
    --statistics Average,Maximum

# 3. Check disk I/O
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
    --namespace AWS/EBS \
    --metric-name VolumeReadOps \
    --dimensions Name=VolumeId,Value=vol-0abc123def456789 \
    --start-time 2024-01-01T00:00:00Z \
    --end-time 2024-01-01T23:59:59Z \
    --period 3600 \
    --statistics Sum

# 4. SSH into instance and run diagnostics
ssh -i anihpj-keypair.pem ec2-user@1.2.3.4

# Check CPU and memory
top -n 1

# Check disk usage
df -h

# Check disk I/O
iostat -x 1 5

# Check network
netstat -an | grep ESTABLISHED | wc -l  # Active connections

# Check Django logs
tail -f /var/log/anihpj/gunicorn.log
tail -f /var/log/anihpj/django.log

# Check slow queries in PostgreSQL
# Connect to RDS and run:
SELECT query, mean_exec_time, calls 
FROM pg_stat_statements 
ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC 
LIMIT 10;
💰 Common FAQs
Q: Why am I being charged when my instance is stopped?
A: Stopped instances don't incur EC2 compute charges, but you're 
   still charged for:
   • EBS volumes attached to the instance
   • Elastic IP addresses not associated with running instances
   • Snapshots and AMIs
   
   Solution: Delete unused EBS volumes, release unused Elastic IPs

Q: What's the difference between Stop and Terminate?
A: • Stop: Instance is stopped, can be started again, data preserved
   • Terminate: Instance is permanently deleted, cannot be recovered
   
   Tip: Enable termination protection for production instances!

Q: Can I change instance type without losing data?
A: Yes! Stop the instance, change instance type, then start it.
   Data on EBS volumes is preserved.
   
   aws ec2 stop-instances --instance-ids i-0abc123
   aws ec2 modify-instance-attribute \
       --instance-id i-0abc123 \
       --instance-type m5.xlarge
   aws ec2 start-instances --instance-ids i-0abc123

Q: How do I transfer files to/from EC2?
A: Multiple options:
   • SCP: scp -i key.pem file.txt ec2-user@1.2.3.4:/home/ec2-user/
   • SFTP: sftp -i key.pem ec2-user@1.2.3.4
   • S3: Upload to S3, then download on EC2 (aws s3 cp)
   • rsync: rsync -avz -e "ssh -i key.pem" ...

Q: What happens to my data when instance is terminated?
A: • Root EBS volume: Deleted (unless DeleteOnTermination=false)
   • Additional EBS volumes: Preserved (unless explicitly deleted)
   • Instance Store: Permanently lost
   
   Always backup important data!

Q: Can I recover a terminated instance?
A: No, termination is permanent. However:
   • If you have EBS snapshots, create new instance from snapshot
   • If you have AMI backup, launch new instance from AMI
   • Check CloudTrail to see who terminated it and when

Q: How do I get a static IP for my EC2 instance?
A: Allocate and associate an Elastic IP:
   
   aws ec2 allocate-address --domain vpc
   aws ec2 associate-address \
       --instance-id i-0abc123 \
       --allocation-id eipalloc-0abc123

Q: Can I use EC2 without a VPC?
A: No, EC2-Classic has been retired. All EC2 instances must be 
   launched in a VPC (Virtual Private Cloud).
